message.content:
Adversarial refuter: ASSUME this finding is FALSE; disprove it from the code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. survives=true only if you genuinely cannot.
P7-TESTQUAL-1: High-stakes (T3) error-classification tests are non-discriminating: assert the base class ReasoningError, so a transient/permanent/rate-limit misclassification still passes
/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/integration/test_reasoning_drain.py:143-171, 202-206
The whole purpose of the T3 drain classifier (src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:491-502) is to map an SDK result error to the CORRECT subtype, because the retry executor branches on the exact subtype: PermanentReasoningError => no retry (seam.py:599), RateLimitError => long backpressure (seam.py:601), base ReasoningError => fast backoff (seam.py:606). Both PermanentReasoningError and RateLimitError subclass ReasoningError (seam.py:66,70,74). Three drain tests assert the classification but only via pytest.raises(ReasoningError) (the base class): test_t3_empty_reply_raises_transient (line 143-146), test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient (a 500, line 168-171), and test_t3_sdk_raises_mid_stream_maps_to_transient (line 202-206). Because pytest.raises catches subclasses, these pass even if the code misclassifies — e.g. a regression that mapped a generic 500 to PermanentReasoningError (don't retry) or RateLimitError would still satisfy pytest.raises(ReasoningError). The transient-vs-permanent distinction these tests claim to guard is exactly what is NOT asserted, so reverting the 'fall through to base ReasoningError' branch to a permanent/rate-limit classification would not be caught. By contrast, the bulk-path classification tests in test_reasoning.py (e.g. test_bulk_complete_classifies_404_as_permanent_by_status:382, test_malformed_params_raise_typed_permanent_error:404) correctly assert the SPECIFIC subtype, confirming this is an isolated weakness rather than an intentional convention. The empty-reply and mid-stream-raise cases are genuinely meant to be the base/transient type (retryable), so the assertion should pin that they are NOT the more-specific subtypes.
PoC: In src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py replace the generic fall-through at line 498 (`raise ReasoningError(...)`) with `raise PermanentReasoningError(...)`. This is a real misclassification: a transient 500 would now be treated as non-retryable. Re-run tests/integration/test_reasoning_drain.py::test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient — it still PASSES because PermanentReasoningError is a subclass of ReasoningError. The test fails to detect that the 500 is no longer retried.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

506	def _attempt(request: ReasoningRequest) -> str:
507	    """Route one request to its tier backend (no retry here)."""
508	    if _truthy("ASSAY_DISABLE_REASONING"):
509	        raise ReasoningError("reasoning is disabled (ASSAY_DISABLE_REASONING)")
510	    p = request.params
511	    system = p.get("system")
512	    model = p.get("model")
513	    json_mode = bool(p.get("_json_mode", False))
514	    json_schema = p.get("json_schema")  # optional caller schema → constrained JSON decoding
515	    # Coerce numeric params behind the typed error contract (#H-004): a malformed temperature/seed
516	    # (e.g. "hot") would otherwise raise a raw ValueError/TypeError that escapes run()/run_json,
517	    # which document only ReasoningError. A bad param is a caller bug, not transient → permanent.
518	    try:
519	        temperature = float(p.get("temperature", 0.0))
520	        seed_raw = p.get("_seed")
521	        seed = int(seed_raw) if seed_raw is not None else None
522	    except (ValueError, TypeError) as exc:
523	        raise PermanentReasoningError(f"malformed reasoning params: {exc}") from exc
524	    if request.tier is StakesTier.BULK:
525	        return cast(
526	            str,
527	            _with_timeout(
528	                lambda: _bulk_complete(
529	                    request.prompt,
530	                    system,
531	                    temperature,
532	                    model or BULK_MODEL,
533	                    json_mode,
534	                    seed=seed,  # already coerced to int|None above (#H-004)
535	                    json_schema=json_schema,
536	                ),
537	                BULK_TIMEOUT + 10,
538	                "bulk tier",
539	            ),
540	        )
541	    if request.tier is StakesTier.HIGH_STAKES:
542	        # The high-stakes tier runs the Claude CLI subprocess (not the in-process Anthropic SDK,
543	        # so no auto-instrumentor covers it) — emit an explicit OpenInference LLM span so Phoenix
544	        # records the model/provider for this otherwise-untraced tier (#2).
545	        llm_attrs = {
546	            "llm.provider": "anthropic",
547	            "llm.system": "anthropic",
548	            "llm.model_name": str(model or HIGH_STAKES_MODEL or "subscription-default"),
549	        }
550	        with _span("reasoning.high_stakes", llm_attrs, kind="LLM"):
551	            return cast(
552	                str,
553	                _with_timeout(
554	                    lambda: _high_stakes_complete(
555	                        request.prompt, system, model or HIGH_STAKES_MODEL
556	                    ),
557	                    HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT + 30,
558	                    "high-stakes tier",
559	                ),
560	            )
561	    raise PermanentReasoningError(f"unknown tier: {request.tier!r}")
562	
563	
564	def _sleep_bounded(secs: float, deadline: float | None) -> None:
565	    """Sleep ``secs``, but never past ``deadline`` — so backoff can't blow an overall budget.
566	
567	    If the deadline has already passed, raise instead of sleeping the full interval, so a long
568	    rate-limit backoff cannot keep a caller blocked well beyond its deadline (#102).
569	    """
570	    if deadline is not None:
571	        remaining = deadline - time.monotonic()
572	        if remaining <= 0:
573	            raise ReasoningError("reasoning deadline exceeded during backoff")
574	        secs = min(secs, remaining)
575	    time.sleep(secs)
576	
577	
578	def _run_with_retries(request: ReasoningRequest, *, deadline: float | None = None) -> str:
579	    """Execute with independent transient and rate-limit retry budgets.
580	
581	    ``deadline`` (monotonic seconds) bounds both the cumulative backoff AND entry into each
582	    retry: once the deadline has passed, no further ``_attempt`` is started (#102, #F-022). It
583	    does not interrupt an attempt already in flight — that is bounded by the per-call timeout —
584	    so total wall-clock is at most ``deadline`` plus one in-flight attempt's timeout, not
585	    ``deadline`` plus the full retry budget of per-call timeouts.
586	    """
587	    transient = 0
588	    rate = 0
589	    attempted = False
590	    while True:
591	        # Gate retry ENTRY on the deadline (#F-022): the first attempt always runs, but a retry is
592	        # not started once the deadline is past — otherwise the per-attempt timeouts could stack
593	        # well beyond the overall budget even though the backoff sleeps were bounded.
594	        if attempted and deadline is not None and time.monotonic() >= deadline:
595	            raise ReasoningError("reasoning deadline exceeded before retry")
596	        attempted = True
597	        try:
598	            return _attempt(request)
599	        except PermanentReasoningError:
600	            raise
601	        except RateLimitError:
602	            if rate >= RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES:
603	                raise
604	            _sleep_bounded(RATE_LIMIT_BACKOFF * (rate + 1), deadline)
605	            rate += 1
606	        except ReasoningError:
607	            if transient >= MAX_RETRIES:
608	                raise
609	            _sleep_bounded(BACKOFF_BASE**transient, deadline)

content.text: The finding is confirmed by the code. Let me verify the exception class hierarchy.

63	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
64	# Errors
65	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
66	class ReasoningError(RuntimeError):
67	    """Transient reasoning failure (eligible for retry)."""
68	
69	
70	class PermanentReasoningError(ReasoningError):
71	    """Non-transient failure — never retried (bad model, auth, saturation)."""
72	
73	
74	class RateLimitError(ReasoningError):
75	    """Rate-limit / capacity signal — retried with long backpressure, not fast backoff."""
76	
77	
78	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
79	# Config (dataset-agnostic env contract, ASSAY_* prefix)

content.text:
The finding is fully confirmed by the code. Let me try to disprove it by checking whether the three named tests have any additional discriminating assertion, or whether the retry executor somehow distinguishes types in a way that would catch a misclassification through these tests. The code is clear:

1. Class hierarchy (seam.py:66,70,74): `PermanentReasoningError` and `RateLimitError` both subclass `ReasoningError`. Confirmed.

2. The three tests assert only `pytest.raises(ReasoningError)`:
- `test_t3_empty_reply_raises_transient` (143-146)
- `test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient` (168-171, a 500)
- `test_t3_sdk_raises_mid_stream_maps_to_transient` (202-206)

3. Because `pytest.raises` catches subclasses, a misclassification to `PermanentReasoningError` (no retry, seam.py:599) or `RateLimitError` (long backpressure, seam.py:601) would still satisfy `pytest.raises(ReasoningError)`. The PoC (changing line 498 to `PermanentReasoningError`) would indeed still pass `test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient`.

4. The contrast holds: sibling tests in the same file (e.g. `test_t3_auth_error_maps_to_permanent`:164, `test_t3_overloaded_529_maps_to_ratelimit`:184) DO assert the specific subtype, and the finding's cited bulk-path tests do too — so this is an isolated weakness, not a deliberate convention.

I cannot disprove this. The three tests genuinely do not pin the negative (that the error is NOT a more-specific subtype), so a regression reverting the transient fall-through to permanent/rate-limit classification would pass undetected.

input.reasoning: Confirmed from code; could not disprove. Class hierarchy at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:66,70,74 shows PermanentReasoningError(66->70) and RateLimitError(74) both subclass ReasoningError. The retry executor branches on exact subtype: PermanentReasoningError -> no retry (seam.py:599), RateLimitError -> long backpressure (seam.py:601-604), base ReasoningError -> fast backoff (seam.py:606-609). Three drain tests assert only pytest.raises(ReasoningError), the base class: test_t3_empty_reply_raises_transient (test_reasoning_drain.py:143-146), test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient (a 500; 168-171), test_t3_sdk_raises_mid_stream_maps_to_transient (202-206). Since pytest.raises catches subclasses, these pass even under misclassification. PoC verified by inspection: changing seam.py:498 from `raise ReasoningError(...)` to `raise PermanentReasoningError(...)` (a real regression making a transient 500 non-retryable) still satisfies pytest.raises(ReasoningError) in test_t3_generic_error_maps_to_transient, so the transient-vs-permanent distinction the test names is not actually guarded. The contrast is real: sibling tests in the SAME file assert the specific subtype where it matters (test_t3_auth_error_maps_to_permanent:164 -> PermanentReasoningError, test_t3_overloaded_529_maps_to_ratelimit:184 and test_t3_rate_limit_event_rejected_maps_to_ratelimit:151 -> RateLimitError), and the cited bulk-path tests in test_reasoning.py assert specific subtypes too — confirming an isolated weakness, not an intentional convention. The three transient cases are genuinely meant to be retryable base/transient errors, so the assertions should additionally pin that they are NOT PermanentReasoningError / RateLimitError. No mitigating mechanism elsewhere catches this. Finding stands.

content.text: Finding survives. The three high-stakes (T3) drain tests assert only the base class `ReasoningError`, which catches all subclasses, so a regression misclassifying a transient error as `PermanentReasoningError` (non-retryable) or `RateLimitError` would pass undetected — exactly the distinction these tests claim to guard. I confirmed this against the class hierarchy and retry-branching logic in `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py` and the test bodies in `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/integration/test_reasoning_drain.py`.
